attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with symptoms of acute bowel obstruction secondary to an incarcerated inguinal hernia requiring an urgent hernia repair. Two days after the procedure he has evidence of a paralytic ileus, is unable to tolerate food and still requires intravenous fluids. In the previous 24 hours there has been 1.5L of nasogastric aspirate and 1.2L urine output. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 123/min and blood pressure 118/62mmHg. He has dry mucous membrane with no signs of fluid overload or shock. His admission weight was noted at 70kg. His serum biochemistry shows that his electrolyte values are within normal limits with a normal kidney function.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate intravenous fluid regimen for the next 24 hours?
attributes.explanation:
The normal maintenance fluid for a 70kg man will be 2-3L in 24 hours. His daily sodium and potassium requirements should be both 70mmol and glucose requirement of 50-100g/day. Since his renal function is normal, his fluid management can be easily given in the form of either crystalloids: 1 L of isotonic (0.9%) saline (154 mmol sodium) or 1 L Hartmann's solution (129mmol sodium and 5mmol potassium) or 1 L of dextrose saline, with 30 mmol of KCL added to each litre of fluid. He will also need some replacement fluid. In this instance 1.5 L of gastric contents have been aspirated and should be replaced with a crystalloid solution and potassium bags to reduce hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis. Thus he requires at least 3 L of fluid in the next 24 hours. He has a good urine output with normal renal function, so the potassium supplements can be given safely.

3L of 0.9% Sodium Chloride is an incorrect answer as this type of crystalloid solution has nil potassium for replacement and this patient requires further replacement of potassium via nasogastric losses. A common side effect from large volumes of NaCl solution is hyperchloremic acidosis.

Provided that patient's sodium level is normal and not septic. Dextrose solution is added to stimulate basal insulin secretion and prevent muscle breakdown when the patient is unable to eat or drink. However, the 2L Dextrose solution will not replace the potassium deficit via nasogastric losses.

Hartmann's solution is considered to be more physiological than 0.9% Sodium chloride solution as it contains electrolyte concentrations similar to plasma. By giving 2L of Hartmann's solution, patient will be able to replace 10mmol/L of potassium lost and attain daily potassium requirements with 2 mini bags of potassium (60mmol). Hence Option C will be the most correct answer.

2L of 0.9% Sodium chloride and I L dextrose with 30mmol potassium is an incorrect option. Although the sodium chloride solution will ameliorate the alkalotic effect from H+, Cl- losses in nasogastric aspiration, it does not replace the K+ loss and 30mmol of potassium replacement is not enough to meet the daily requirement.

3L of Hartmann's solution although more physiological than saline, just given alone, it does not meet the adult requirements of potassium and calories. Hence, additional potassium and dextrose fluid are required. Hence, this option is incorrect.
learningPoints: Choice of fluid regime should incorporate clinical history and clinical signs of dehydration/shock/fluid overload, a medical history of renal, liver or heart failure and calculating daily maintenance regime and replacing fluid loss.
references: Myles PS, Bellomo R, Corcoran T, et al. Restrictive versus Liberal Fluid Therapy for Major Abdominal Surgery. N Engl J Med. 2018;378:2263. Available at: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1801601
Rassam SS, Counsell DJ. Perioperative electrolyte and fluid balance. Educ Anaesth Crit Care and Pain. 2005;5:157–60. Available at: https://academic.oup.com/bjaed/article/5/5/157/283230

Self WH, Semler MW, Wanderer JP, Wang L, Byrne DW, et al. Balanced Crystalloids versus Saline in Noncritically Ill Adults. N Engl J Med. 2018;378(9):819-828. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5846618/
